title: The Resolved Brief
subtitle: The 20-Minute System That Organizes Your Entire Life For The People You Love.

sections.title: Foundation & Legal
sections.subtitle: If something happened tomorrow, would your wishes be followed — or would the state decide for you?
sections.intro_message: Let's start with the foundation. These questions determine whether your family would follow your wishes — or be left guessing.

questions.prompt: If something happened tomorrow, would your wishes be followed — or decided by the state?
options: My wishes are clearly documented and legally binding
I have some things written down, but it's incomplete
Nothing is documented — the state would decide

questions.prompt: Do you have a will or trust in place?
options: Yes — current and reviewed recently
Yes — but it may be outdated
No

questions.prompt: Does your plan reflect your life today — your current family, assets, and relationships?
options: Fully up to date
Some things have changed since I last updated it
Outdated or never created

questions.prompt: If someone needed your will tonight, would they know where to find it?
options: Yes — it's in a known, accessible location
It exists, but they'd have to search for it
No one would know where to look

questions.prompt: Have you formally assigned someone to handle your affairs — power of attorney, executor, trustee?
options: Yes — legally documented
Informally — I've mentioned it but nothing is filed
No one has been assigned

questions.prompt: Would that person actually know what to do first?
options: Yes — we've discussed it
They'd figure it out eventually
They'd have no idea where to start

questions.prompt: Are your important documents stored in one known location — or scattered?
options: One secure, known location
A few different places
Scattered or I'm not sure where everything is

questions.prompt: Who is your estate attorney — and could your family reach them?
options: I have one and my family knows how to reach them
I have one but my family doesn't know who it is
I don't have one

questions.prompt: If your family had to prove what you wanted — in court — could they?
options: Yes — everything is legally documented
Some things, but not everything
No — nothing would hold up
questions.callout: This is the question most people get wrong. Verbal agreements mean nothing in probate court.

sections.title: Key People & Decision Makers
sections.subtitle: Someone will have to step in. The question is whether they'll be ready — or drowning.
sections.intro_message: Now let's figure out who your people are — and whether they're actually prepared to step in.

questions.prompt: If something happened to you tonight, who would actually step in to handle everything?
options: One person — clearly defined and they know it
I have someone in mind, but we've never discussed it
No one — it would be chaos

questions.prompt: Would that person know where to start in the first 24 hours?
options: Yes — they have a clear starting point
They'd figure it out, but it would take time
They'd be completely lost

questions.prompt: If both you and your partner were gone at the same time, who steps in next?
options: Clearly defined backup
Someone would probably step up
No one — it would be a crisis on top of a crisis

questions.prompt: Have you actually told these people they're the ones?
options: Yes — we've had the conversation
I assumed they'd figure it out
No — I've never said it out loud

questions.prompt: Would your family know who to call for help — your attorney, CPA, financial advisor, insurance agent?
options: Yes — names and numbers are documented
They'd know some, but not all
They wouldn't know who any of these people are

questions.prompt: Would the roles between your key people be clear — or would everyone step on each other?
options: Clear — everyone knows their role
Some overlap, but manageable
It would be confusing and likely cause conflict

questions.prompt: Does your family know your wishes well enough to speak for you confidently?
options: Yes — they could represent my wishes clearly
On some things, but not all
They'd be guessing

questions.prompt: If a disagreement broke out between family members, is there a clear tiebreaker?
options: Yes — one person has final authority, documented
Informally understood
No — it would turn into a fight
questions.callout: Family conflict after a crisis is more common than people think. This one question prevents it.

questions.prompt: Could your key people access what they need — accounts, documents, contacts — within 48 hours?
options: Yes — everything is organized and accessible
Some things, but they'd have to dig
No — they'd be locked out of almost everything

sections.title: Children & Dependents
sections.subtitle: If you weren't here tomorrow, would your children's world keep turning — or fall apart?
sections.intro_message: This section is for parents and caregivers. Skip it if it doesn't apply to you.

questions.prompt: Is there a clear, documented plan for who would care for your children?
options: Yes — legal guardianship is designated
I've thought about it but nothing is official
No plan exists

questions.prompt: Is there a backup guardian if your first choice can't do it?
options: Yes — identified and documented
I have someone in mind
No backup

questions.prompt: Have you told the guardian they've been chosen — and do they understand what it means?
options: Yes — we've had a real conversation about it
I've mentioned it casually
No — they don't know

questions.prompt: Could someone continue your children's daily routines — school, activities, medical, meals?
options: Yes — it's all documented or well-known
Some of it, but there are gaps
They'd be starting from scratch

questions.prompt: Are your children's critical details easy to find — doctors, allergies, medications, school contacts?
options: Yes — organized and accessible
Scattered across different places
Only in my head

questions.prompt: Would someone know how to emotionally support your children through this — therapist contacts, special needs, comfort routines?
options: Yes — documented or well-communicated
Partially
No — no one would know what they need

questions.prompt: Would your children's future — education, values, traditions — be guided by your wishes or guessed at?
options: Guided by my clearly stated wishes
Somewhat — people close to me would try
Mostly guessed
questions.callout: This is the one that keeps parents up at night. If you haven't written it down, you're leaving your children's future to interpretation.

questions.prompt: Are there financial provisions specifically for your children — education funds, trusts, designated accounts?
options: Yes — clearly set up and documented
Some, but incomplete
Nothing specific

sections.title: Money, Assets & Obligations
sections.subtitle: Bills don't stop. Mortgages don't pause. Could your family keep the lights on?
sections.intro_message: Now let's look at the money. Not the numbers — just whether your family could find it and manage it without you.

questions.prompt: If your family needed money within 7 days, would they know exactly where to get it?
options: Yes — clear access to accounts
They'd eventually figure it out
They'd have no idea where to start

questions.prompt: How easy would it be for someone to find ALL of your accounts — banking, retirement, investments, crypto?
options: Everything documented in one place
Some are documented, others aren't
It would be a scavenger hunt

questions.prompt: Are there accounts your family doesn't even know exist?
options: No — they know about everything
Possibly one or two
Yes — definitely accounts they'd never find
questions.callout: Billions in retirement funds, life insurance, and bank accounts go unclaimed every year. This is why.

questions.prompt: Could someone keep your bills running — mortgage, utilities, insurance premiums, subscriptions?
options: Yes — everything is documented with autopay details
They'd figure out most of it
Bills would start failing within weeks

questions.prompt: Are your debts and obligations clearly outlined — mortgage, car loans, student loans, credit cards?
options: Yes — all documented
Some are known, others aren't
My family would discover debts they didn't know about

questions.prompt: Would someone know what you own — property, vehicles, investments, business interests?
options: Yes — a clear inventory exists
They'd know the big things but miss others
They'd have no idea what I own

questions.prompt: If you have business interests or side income, could someone manage or wind them down?
options: Yes — documented with instructions
Partially — some knowledge exists
No — it would collapse or be abandoned
N/A

questions.prompt: Would someone know who to contact for financial help — your banker, advisor, accountant?
options: Yes — names and contacts documented
They'd know one or two
They wouldn't know who to call

questions.prompt: Do beneficiary designations on your accounts match your current wishes?
options: Yes — recently reviewed and current
I'm not sure — haven't checked in a while
I don't know what's listed
questions.callout: Beneficiary designations override your will. An ex-spouse on a 401k gets the money — even if your will says otherwise.

questions.prompt: Could your family handle your full financial picture — income, expenses, debts, assets — without major confusion?
options: Yes — it's organized and accessible
With effort and time
It would be overwhelming

sections.title: Insurance & Protection
sections.subtitle: Insurance only works if someone files a claim. If they don't know it exists, it's worthless.
sections.intro_message: Let's look at your safety net. Not just what you have — but whether your family could actually use it.

questions.prompt: Do you have life insurance?
options: Yes — through work, private policy, or both
I think so, but I'm not sure of the details
No

questions.prompt: If a life insurance claim needed to be filed, would someone know how?
options: Yes — provider, policy number, and claims process documented
They'd know I have it but would have to figure out the rest
No one would know where to start

questions.prompt: Do you know your actual coverage amount — and would it cover your family for at least 2 years?
options: Yes — I know the amount and it's adequate
I know the amount but it might not be enough
I have no idea what my coverage is

questions.prompt: Are your other insurance policies documented — health, home, auto, disability, umbrella?
options: Yes — all policies organized in one place
Some are, some aren't
My family would have to call around to find them

questions.prompt: Do you have disability insurance — and do you know what it covers?
options: Yes — through work or privately, and I understand the coverage
I think I have it through work, but I'm not sure
No / I don't know

questions.prompt: If you were incapacitated for 6 months, would your income be replaced?
options: Yes — disability coverage would kick in
Partially — some coverage but gaps exist
No — my family would be on their own financially
questions.callout: Most people insure their car but not their income. Your ability to earn is your most valuable asset.

questions.prompt: Could someone locate your insurance agent or broker?
options: Yes — contact info is documented
My family might be able to find them
No one knows who they are

questions.prompt: Are beneficiaries on your insurance policies current — not an ex-spouse, deceased parent, or outdated name?
options: Yes — recently verified
I'm not sure — haven't checked
Likely outdated

sections.title: Digital Life & Access
sections.subtitle: Your entire life is behind a passcode. If no one can get in, everything is lost.
sections.intro_message: Your phone, email, and passwords control everything. Let's see if your family could get in.

questions.prompt: Could someone you trust unlock your phone today?
options: Yes — they know the passcode
Maybe — I use biometrics and haven't shared the backup PIN
No — only I can get in

questions.prompt: If your email is the key to everything — password resets, bank statements, account recovery — could anyone get into it?
options: Yes — access is documented
They might be able to figure it out
No — it's completely locked

questions.prompt: Where are your passwords stored?
options: Password manager — and someone knows the master password
Scattered — some saved in browser, some on paper, some in my head
Only in my head

questions.prompt: Could your family get into your computer?
options: Yes — they know the password
Maybe — it's usually unlocked
No — it's locked and no one has the password

questions.prompt: Would your family be able to access your photos, videos, and irreplaceable digital memories?
options: Yes — they know how to access my cloud storage
Some things, but not everything
No — decades of memories would be gone
questions.callout: Photos can't be recovered. If they're locked in iCloud or Google Photos behind a password no one has, they're gone forever.

questions.prompt: Are important documents stored in the cloud — and could your family access them?
options: Yes — Google Drive, Dropbox, iCloud — and access is documented
Some are in the cloud, but access isn't clear
I don't store things in the cloud / no one could get in

questions.prompt: Do you have cryptocurrency, NFTs, or digital assets that require a private key or recovery phrase?
options: Yes — and recovery info is securely stored where someone could find it
Yes — but the recovery phrase is only known to me
No digital assets like this

questions.prompt: What financial apps are on your phone — and could someone access them?
options: Documented — banking, Venmo, PayPal, investment apps are all known
They'd find some but miss others
No one would know what apps I use for money

questions.prompt: If every digital account you have was locked tomorrow, how much of your life would be inaccessible?
options: Very little — most things are documented offline too
A significant amount
Almost everything — my entire life is digital

sections.title: Medical & Final Wishes
sections.subtitle: When you can't speak for yourself, someone else will. Have you told them what to say?
sections.intro_message: Last section. This is where logistics meets legacy. Take your time.

questions.prompt: If you couldn't speak for yourself, who is making medical decisions — and do they know it?
options: Clearly defined, legally documented, and they know
Informally understood but nothing is legal
No one has been chosen

questions.prompt: Would that person know what you actually want — life support, resuscitation, organ donation?
options: Yes — we've had the conversation and it's written down
We've talked about it loosely
They'd be guessing in a hospital hallway

questions.prompt: Is your medical history accessible — conditions, medications, allergies, doctors?
options: Yes — documented and easy to find
Partially — my spouse knows most of it
Only I know my full medical picture

questions.prompt: Would your family feel confident making medical decisions on your behalf — without guilt or doubt?
options: Yes — my wishes are clear enough to remove the burden
Somewhat — but they'd still second-guess
No — they'd carry that weight forever
questions.callout: The guilt of making a wrong medical decision for a loved one never goes away. Clarity is the kindest thing you can give them.

questions.prompt: Have you expressed how you want to be remembered — the values, traditions, and words that matter most to you?
options: Yes — written down
I've mentioned some things in conversation
No — I've never put it into words

questions.prompt: Would your funeral or memorial preferences be followed — or argued about?
options: Followed — clearly documented
Mostly followed — people have a general idea
Argued about — no one knows what I'd want

questions.prompt: Is there anything you want your family to know — a message, a letter, something only you can say — that you haven't written down yet?
options: I've written it
I've thought about it but haven't done it
I haven't — but I should
questions.callout: This is the last question for a reason. Everything above is logistics. This is legacy. Don't leave the most important thing unsaid.
